metadata.lab: Holberton Cybersecurity — Purple Team Adversary Emulation Lab

scoring_system.noise: 1 (silent, zero false positives) to 5 (constant false positives)

environment.emulation_framework: Atomic Red Team v2.1.0 + MITRE Caldera 5.2.0

rules_fired.description: Powershell.exe spawned a powershell process which executed a base64 encoded command
T1059.001 - PowerShell base64 encoded command execution detected

scores.confidence_rationale: Fired on every test run, caught both the cmd-proxied and direct invocation paths
scores.noise_rationale: Zero false positives observed — encoded PowerShell is rare in normal VM operation

gaps: Alternate encoding flags not in regex (e.g. -ec) would evade
pwsh.exe on newer systems covered by separate regex entry

techniques.technique_name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder

rules_fired.description: Registry entry to be executed on next logon was modified using reg.exe
rules_fired.note: Level too low — raised by custom rule 100003

rules_fired.description: T1547.001 - Registry Run key persistence detected (level raised from 6)

scores.confidence_rationale: Fired on every test run — EID 13 is reliably generated for any reg.exe Run key write
scores.noise_rationale: Software installers occasionally write Run keys during install — msiexec filtered out but other installers may trigger

gaps: PowerShell Set-ItemProperty writing Run keys bypasses rule 92302 but still hits 100003 via EID 13
HKLM Run key modifications covered by same contains pattern

techniques.test_command: cmd.exe /c net user & net localgroup Users & net localgroup

rules_fired.description: A net.exe binary was started by a Windows cmd shell
rules_fired.note: Wrong MITRE mapping — T1059.003 and T1574.001 instead of T1087.001. Fixed by custom rule 100004

rules_fired.description: T1087.001 - Local account discovery via net.exe (corrected MITRE mapping)

detection.detection_signal: net.exe or net1.exe with user or localgroup subcommand

scores.confidence_rationale: Fired on every test run — all three net.exe invocations caught separately
scores.noise_rationale: net user and net localgroup are common legitimate admin commands — medium false positive risk in production environments, acceptable in this lab

gaps: PowerShell Get-LocalUser and Get-LocalGroupMember not covered
LDAP queries for domain account discovery not covered

detection.week2_status: UNDETECTED — zero EID 10 events, no rules existed
detection.week3_fix: Added ProcessAccess rule to Sysmon config for lsass.exe, wrote rules 100005 and 100006

rules_fired.description: T1003.001 - LSASS dump file created (Out-Minidump credential dumping detected)
T1003.001 - Process accessed lsass.exe memory (possible credential dumping)
rules_fired.detection_signal: powershell.exe opened lsass.exe with GrantedAccess 0x1F3FFF and 0x1FFFFF

scores.confidence_rationale: Two complementary rules provide dual coverage — EID 11 catches the dump file artifact, EID 10 catches the memory access event. Both fired on test run
scores.noise_rationale: Three false positive sources identified and suppressed via negate filter (VBoxService, wazuh-agent, MicrosoftEdgeUpdate). Task Manager alerts intentionally kept

gaps: Mimikatz sekurlsa::logonpasswords — EID 10 would catch it via different GrantedAccess value
Direct syscall credential dumping tools may bypass Sysmon EID 10 entirely
Procdump.exe dumping lsass — EID 11 would catch the .dmp file
false_positives_suppressed: VBoxService.exe — VirtualBox guest service accesses lsass every 15 seconds

critical_gaps_closed: T1003.001 — completely undetected in Week 2, now covered by 2 rules at level 15
T1087.001 — wrong MITRE mapping corrected from T1059.003 to T1087.001
T1547.001 — severity level raised from 6 to 12
remaining_gaps: PowerShell-based account enumeration evades T1087.001 rule
Direct syscall credential dumping tools evade Sysmon EID 10
